umbrellas.label: Simple Harmonic Motion
umbrellas.description: Oscillatory motion where the restoring force is proportional to displacement, covering mass-spring and pendulum systems.

intro.label: Simple Harmonic Motion Overview
intro.description: Simple harmonic motion (SHM) occurs when a restoring force is proportional to displacement, causing sinusoidal oscillations. The two canonical systems are the mass-spring oscillator and the simple pendulum, each with a characteristic period formula. Understanding amplitude, period, and energy in SHM sets the foundation for all wave phenomena.

in_depth.label: Amplitude, frequency, and phase in SHM
in_depth.description: Identify amplitude as the maximum displacement, frequency $f$ as oscillations per second (Hz), and phase as the initial position in the cycle; relate period and frequency by $T = 1/f$. This focuses on the descriptive parameters of oscillation, not on calculating the period from system properties (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Mass-spring period and frequency
in_depth.description: Calculate the period of a mass-spring oscillator using $T = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$, noting that period is independent of amplitude and depends only on mass and spring constant. This focuses on the mass-spring system, not the simple pendulum (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Simple pendulum period and frequency
in_depth.description: Calculate the period of a simple pendulum using $T = 2\pi\sqrt{L/g}$, noting it is independent of mass and amplitude (for small angles). This focuses on the pendulum, not the mass-spring system (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Energy in simple harmonic motion
in_depth.description: Describe the interchange between kinetic energy ($\frac{1}{2}mv^2$) and elastic potential energy ($\frac{1}{2}kx^2$) in SHM, with total mechanical energy $E = \frac{1}{2}kA^2$ constant (no damping). This focuses on SHM energy conservation, not the spring PE formula in isolation (which lives in P4).
examples: max KE at equilibrium; max PE at amplitude

umbrellas.label: Wave Properties & Classification
umbrellas.description: Fundamental properties shared by all mechanical waves — wave types, the wave relation, and superposition.

intro.label: Wave Properties Overview
intro.description: Waves transfer energy through a medium without net transport of matter. They are classified as transverse (displacement perpendicular to propagation) or longitudinal (displacement parallel to propagation). The universal wave relation $v = f\lambda$ connects propagation speed, frequency, and wavelength; superposition governs what happens when waves overlap.

in_depth.label: Transverse and longitudinal waves
in_depth.description: Classify a wave as transverse (particle displacement perpendicular to propagation direction, e.g., a plucked string) or longitudinal (particle displacement parallel to propagation direction, e.g., sound). This focuses on wave type classification, not on wave speed or frequency (separate keywords).

in_depth.label: Wavelength and propagation speed
in_depth.description: Define wavelength $\lambda$ as the spatial repeat distance of a wave and apply the wave relation $v = f\lambda$ to relate propagation speed, frequency, and wavelength. This focuses on the $v = f\lambda$ relation, not on what determines the speed in a given medium (a separate keyword).
examples: higher frequency → shorter wavelength at fixed speed

in_depth.label: Superposition and interference
in_depth.description: Apply the principle of superposition — the net displacement at any point equals the algebraic sum of individual wave displacements — and distinguish constructive interference (amplitudes add) from destructive interference (amplitudes cancel). This focuses on the superposition principle, not on standing waves as a specific interference pattern (a separate keyword).
examples: constructive: waves in phase → amplitude doubles
destructive: waves 180° out of phase → cancel
net displacement = sum of individual displacements

umbrellas.label: Sound Production & Speed
umbrellas.description: How sound is produced as a mechanical longitudinal wave and how its propagation speed varies with medium.

intro.label: Sound Production & Speed Overview
intro.description: Sound is a mechanical longitudinal pressure wave that requires a medium — it cannot travel through a vacuum. It is produced by vibrating objects that create periodic compressions and rarefactions. The speed of sound depends on the medium's stiffness and density, making it fastest in solids and slowest in gases.

in_depth.label: Production of sound
in_depth.description: Describe how a vibrating object (string, membrane, vocal cord) creates alternating compressions and rarefactions in the surrounding medium, generating a longitudinal sound wave. This focuses on the mechanical origin of sound, not on its speed or intensity (separate keywords).
examples: vibrating tuning fork compresses + rarifies air
sound needs a medium — no vacuum propagation
frequency of source = frequency of sound produced

in_depth.label: Relative speed of sound in solids, liquids, and gases
in_depth.description: Rank the speed of sound as greatest in solids, intermediate in liquids, and lowest in gases, because speed increases with greater stiffness (bulk/Young's modulus) and decreases with greater density. This focuses on the relative ranking across phases, not on the precise numerical values (which are problem-specific stems).
examples: greater stiffness of medium → higher sound speed

umbrellas.label: Sound Intensity & Decibels
umbrellas.description: Quantitative treatment of sound intensity and the logarithmic decibel scale.

intro.label: Sound Intensity & Decibels Overview
intro.description: Sound intensity is the power per unit area carried by a sound wave; it falls off with distance and is measured on a logarithmic decibel scale because the human ear spans a vast dynamic range. The log scale means that a 10 dB increase corresponds to a tenfold increase in intensity. Attenuation describes how intensity decreases as sound travels through a real medium.

in_depth.label: Sound intensity
in_depth.description: Define sound intensity $I$ as power per unit area (W/m²) and recognize that it decreases with the square of distance from a point source ($I \propto 1/r^2$). This focuses on the physical quantity of intensity, not on the decibel conversion (a separate keyword).
examples: doubling distance → intensity drops by factor of 4

in_depth.label: Decibel scale and logarithmic sound level
in_depth.description: Calculate sound level in decibels using $\beta = 10\log_{10}(I / I_0)$ where $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m², and interpret a 10 dB change as a tenfold change in intensity. This focuses on the log-scale conversion, not on what intensity represents physically (a separate keyword).
examples: 0 dB = threshold of hearing; 120 dB = pain threshold

in_depth.label: Attenuation and damping of sound
in_depth.description: Describe attenuation as the decrease in sound intensity with distance due to absorption by the medium and geometric spreading, and damping as the decay of oscillation amplitude over time due to energy dissipation. This focuses on energy loss mechanisms, not on the decibel scale (a separate keyword).
examples: absorption converts acoustic energy to heat
damping: amplitude decays exponentially over time
soft materials attenuate sound more than rigid ones

umbrellas.label: Doppler Effect
umbrellas.description: The observed shift in frequency when source and/or observer are in relative motion.

intro.label: Doppler Effect Overview
intro.description: The Doppler effect is the change in observed frequency (and pitch) when a sound source and an observer move relative to each other. Approach compresses wavefronts (higher observed frequency); recession stretches them (lower observed frequency). The same physics explains sonar reflection from moving objects, which gives a double Doppler shift.
examples: reflection from moving object: double Doppler shift

in_depth.label: Doppler effect — moving source
in_depth.description: Predict the observed frequency when a sound source moves toward or away from a stationary observer using $f_{obs} = f_s \frac{v}{v \mp v_s}$ (minus for approach, plus for recession), noting that wavefronts are compressed ahead of the source and stretched behind it. This focuses on a moving source, not a moving observer (a separate keyword).
examples: ambulance siren pitch drops as it passes

in_depth.label: Doppler effect — moving observer
in_depth.description: Predict the observed frequency when an observer moves toward or away from a stationary source using $f_{obs} = f_s \frac{v \pm v_o}{v}$ (plus for approach, minus for recession). This focuses on observer motion, not source motion (a separate keyword).
examples: observer motion changes encounter rate with wavefronts

in_depth.label: Doppler effect from reflection off a moving object
in_depth.description: Analyze the double Doppler shift when sound reflects from a moving object: the object acts first as a moving observer (receiving a shifted frequency) and then as a moving source (re-emitting at that shifted frequency), yielding a larger net frequency shift used in Doppler ultrasound and radar. This focuses on the reflection case, not on direct source/observer motion (covered in separate keywords).
examples: double Doppler: object = moving observer then moving source
net shift larger than single-motion case

in_depth.label: Pitch and frequency
in_depth.description: Relate pitch (the perceptual attribute of highness or lowness of a sound) to the physical frequency of the sound wave: higher frequency → higher pitch. This focuses on the pitch-frequency connection, not on the mechanisms that shift frequency such as the Doppler effect (covered elsewhere).
examples: musical notes correspond to specific frequencies (e.g., A4 = 440 Hz)

umbrellas.label: Standing Waves, Resonance & Harmonics
umbrellas.description: Standing wave patterns in strings and pipes, resonant frequencies, and the harmonic series.

intro.label: Standing Waves & Resonance Overview
intro.description: Standing waves form when two identical waves traveling in opposite directions interfere, producing nodes (zero displacement) and antinodes (maximum displacement). Resonance occurs at specific frequencies — the natural frequencies — where the boundary conditions of the system (string fixed at both ends; pipe open or closed) are satisfied. Each resonant frequency is a harmonic of the fundamental.
examples: nodes = zero displacement; antinodes = max displacement
open pipe: all harmonics; closed pipe: odd harmonics only

in_depth.label: Standing waves — nodes and antinodes
in_depth.description: Identify nodes (points of permanent zero displacement) and antinodes (points of maximum displacement amplitude) in a standing wave, and explain that they arise from the superposition of two waves of equal frequency traveling in opposite directions. This focuses on identifying the pattern, not on the resonant frequencies of specific systems (separate keywords).
examples: fixed end → node; open end → antinode
two oppositely-traveling waves of same $f$ and $A$ interfere

in_depth.label: Resonance in strings — harmonic series
in_depth.description: Calculate the resonant (harmonic) frequencies of a string fixed at both ends using $f_n = n\frac{v}{2L}$ ($n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$), where $v$ is the wave speed on the string and $L$ is the string length; all harmonics (odd and even) are present. This focuses on fixed-fixed strings, not on open or closed pipes (separate keywords).
examples: all harmonics present in fixed-fixed string

in_depth.label: Resonance in open pipes — harmonic series
in_depth.description: Calculate the resonant frequencies of an open cylindrical pipe (open at both ends) using $f_n = n\frac{v}{2L}$ ($n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$), where antinodes form at both open ends; all harmonics are present. This focuses on the open-pipe case, not the closed-pipe case (a separate keyword).
examples: flute is approximately an open pipe

in_depth.label: Resonance in closed pipes — odd harmonics only
in_depth.description: Calculate the resonant frequencies of a pipe closed at one end using $f_n = n\frac{v}{4L}$ ($n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$, odd integers only), where a node forms at the closed end and an antinode at the open end; only odd harmonics are present. This focuses on the closed-pipe case, not the open-pipe case (a separate keyword).
examples: node at closed end; antinode at open end
clarinet (conical closed) supports odd harmonics predominantly

in_depth.label: Beats
in_depth.description: Predict the beat frequency heard when two sound waves of slightly different frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ superpose: $f_{beat} = |f_1 - f_2|$, producing periodic variations in loudness. This focuses on the beat phenomenon, not on standing-wave harmonics (separate keywords).
examples: two forks at 440 Hz and 443 Hz → 3 beats/s

umbrellas.label: Ultrasound & Shock Waves
umbrellas.description: High-frequency sound applications and the physics of supersonic shock waves.

intro.label: Ultrasound & Shock Waves Overview
intro.description: Ultrasound uses frequencies above 20 kHz for medical imaging and therapy, exploiting reflection at tissue interfaces (acoustic impedance mismatch). Shock waves arise when a source exceeds the speed of sound, creating a cone-shaped pressure front (Mach cone) with destructive pressure discontinuities. Both phenomena are medically and physiologically relevant on the MCAT.
examples: ultrasound: $f > 20$ kHz, reflects at tissue interfaces
shock wave: conical wave front from supersonic source

in_depth.label: Ultrasound — principles and medical use
in_depth.description: Describe ultrasound as sound with frequency above 20 kHz that reflects at boundaries between tissues of different acoustic impedance ($Z = \rho v$), enabling echo-based imaging; Doppler ultrasound uses frequency shifts to measure blood-flow velocity. This focuses on ultrasound physics, not on general Doppler theory (a separate keyword).
examples: $f > 20$ kHz; reflects at tissue acoustic-impedance boundaries
Doppler ultrasound measures blood-flow velocity via frequency shift

in_depth.label: Shock waves and the Mach cone
in_depth.description: Explain that a shock wave forms when a source travels at or above the speed of sound ($v_{source} \geq v_{sound}$), creating a conical Mach cone whose half-angle satisfies $\sin\theta = v_{sound}/v_{source}$; the resulting pressure discontinuity carries large amounts of energy. This focuses on supersonic shock waves, not the standard Doppler effect for subsonic motion (a separate keyword).
examples: sonic boom = shock wave reaching an observer
